one_line_takeaway: Introducing the first biomedical MLLM unlearning benchmark (MLLMU-Med) and a unified Unlearning Efficiency Score, the paper finds that five existing unlearning methods trade off forgetting quality against retained capability inconsistently across privacy-leakage and incorrect-fact scenarios, with none succeeding at both, and none meaningfully suppressing leakage on closed-ended questions.
key_finding: On the privacy-disclosure dataset (Table 1), the finetuned model's ROUGE increased by 51.7% (forget set) and 69.17% (retain set) over the base model, confirming successful memorization of injected private data. After unlearning: GA and LLMU achieved only ~7% ROUGE decrease on the forget set (weak forgetting); GD and KL-Min achieved 26.82% and 21.46% ROUGE decreases respectively; IDK achieved the best forgetting, a 45.62% ROUGE decrease on the forget set (nearly matching base-model level) while preserving retain/test performance, giving it the highest UES. Closed-ended (yes/no) accuracy on the forget set stayed comparable to or higher than the finetuned model across all methods, indicating unlearning did not meaningfully suppress privacy leakage on binary-answer questions. On the incorrect-facts dataset (Table 2), results were markedly worse: GD had the lowest forget-set scores (ROUGE 16.09%, BLEU 1.29%) but caused severe collateral damage, with BLEU dropping 27.11% (retain) and 14.3% (test), yielding a negative UES (-0.29); GA, KL-Min, and LLMU preserved retain/test performance but barely reduced forget-set scores (inadequate forgetting); IDK, the best method on the privacy task, showed little effect here (stable ROUGE/BLEU pre/post-unlearning). The ablations showed UES improving over three sequential unlearning rounds for IDK (0.86->1.44->1.53) and LLMU (0.86->1.14->1.46), and UES staying mostly above 0.8 across forget ratios 5-30% despite substantial variance in raw forget-set scores.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) MLLMU-Med, the first multimodal biomedical machine-unlearning benchmark, built via a novel two-stage self-calibrated data-generation pipeline that injects synthetic patient privacy data (via the Python Faker library plus GPT-4o rewriting) into SLAKE VQA pairs, and injects GPT-4o-generated incorrect/outdated diagnostic facts into MM-Skin VQA pairs; (2) the Unlearning Efficiency Score (UES), a single unified metric combining a performance-gap term (retain+test ROUGE minus forget ROUGE) with three penalty terms that punish degradation on retain/test sets or improvement on the forget set after unlearning; (3) an empirical benchmarking study evaluating five existing unlearning algorithms (GA, GD, KL-Min, IDK, LLMU) on LLaVA-Med-V1.5-7B-Mistral finetuned via LoRA, across both scenarios, plus two ablations (sequential/continual unlearning requests, and forget-ratio variation).
threat_model: Not an adversarial attacker/defender threat model. The paper frames two accidental data-hygiene security risks in biomedical MLLM training pipelines: (1) Privacy disclosure -- patient-identifying information (names, emails, phone numbers, appointment dates) is inadvertently incorporated into training VQA pairs (e.g. from web scraping or careless dataset construction), causing the finetuned model to later reproduce that private data verbatim when queried on the same images. (2) Incorrect facts -- unverified or outdated biomedical knowledge from unreliable web sources or old textbooks gets embedded in training data, causing the finetuned model to output factually wrong or unsafe diagnostic content. In both cases the 'attacker' is really unintentional data contamination, not a malicious actor, and the proposed mitigation is post-hoc machine unlearning applied to a model already finetuned on the contaminated data (not training-time filtering or inference-time defense).
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces (explicitly or via its results) the following limitations: no single evaluated method achieves both strong forgetting and preserved capability consistently across both scenarios -- IDK excels at privacy forgetting but fails on incorrect-facts forgetting, illustrating task-dependent brittleness. GD forgets aggressively but destroys retain/test utility (negative UES on incorrect-facts). Unlearning methods leave closed-ended (yes/no) accuracy on the forget set largely unchanged, meaning privacy or incorrect-fact leakage may persist for binary-answer queries even when open-ended ROUGE/BLEU drop. Privacy and incorrect-fact injection are entirely synthetic (Faker-generated fake identities, GPT-4o-generated wrong facts), not real-world contaminated data, and quality filtering relies on GPT-4o's own similarity ratings, introducing potential circularity/bias from using GPT-4o both to generate and to grade data quality. Only five unlearning baselines from prior general-domain LLM/MLLM literature are tested; no unlearning method proposed specifically for biomedical or multimodal settings is introduced. Evaluation is on a single base model (LLaVA-Med-V1.5-7B-Mistral) with LoRA finetuning, so generalization to other MLLM architectures or full-parameter finetuning is untested.

datasets_used: SLAKE (English VQA subset) - source for Privacy Disclosure scenario
MM-Skin (dermatology VQA) - source for Incorrect Facts scenario
MLLMU-Med (this paper's constructed benchmark): Privacy Disclosure forget/retain/test = 351/3960/1500 (total 5811); Incorrect Facts forget/retain/test = 300/3000/1500 (total 4800)
quantitative_results: Privacy task, forget-set ROUGE decrease after unlearning: IDK 45.62% (best), GD 26.82%, KL-Min 21.46%, GA ~7%, LLMU ~7%
Privacy task: finetuned vs base ROUGE increase of 51.7% (forget set) and 69.17% (retain set), showing successful memorization pre-unlearning
Incorrect-facts task (Table 2): GD forget-set ROUGE 16.09%, BLEU 1.29% (best forgetting) but retain/test BLEU dropped 27.11% and 14.3% respectively, UES = -0.29
Incorrect-facts task: IDK forget-set ROUGE/BLEU stayed near finetuned-model levels (48.87%/27.97%), i.e. little forgetting effect despite being the top method on the privacy task
Sequential unlearning ablation (privacy task, IDK): UES rose 0.86 -> 1.44 -> 1.53 across rounds 1-3; LLMU rose 0.86 -> 1.14 -> 1.46
Forget-ratio ablation: UES stayed mostly above 0.8 across ratios from 5% to 30% despite variance in raw forget-set ROUGE
baselines_compared: 'I Don't Know' Tuning (IDK) - Maini et al. 2024
Large Language Model Unlearning (LLMU, KL-Min + IDK) - Yao, Xu & Liu 2024
